questions.text: With reference to the history of India, the term 'Agrahara' primarily refers to:
questions.option_a: A tax-free land grant given to Brahmins for scholarly and religious purposes.
questions.option_b: A revenue-free land grant made to a temple or deity for the maintenance of religious worship.
questions.option_c: A rent-free land grant made to Jain and Buddhist religious institutions or monasteries.
questions.option_d: A land grant made for the maintenance of a school or educational institution.
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Option a is correct: The term Agrahara refers to a revenue-free or tax-exempt village or land grant given to Brahmins
by kings or local rulers, especially during the ancient and early medieval period (c. 600–1200 CE). These grants were
made as a mark of religious merit and patronage of learning.
Key features of an Agrahara:
● The land (often an entire village) was exempted from taxes and state dues.
● It was usually granted to learned Brahmins proficient in the Vedas and sacred texts.
● Such settlements often became centres of Sanskrit learning, Vedic study, and education.
● The Brahmin beneficiaries often enjoyed the right to collect these dues from the local people.
● Agraharas were especially common under South Indian dynasties such as the Pallavas, Chalukyas, and Cholas.
Option b is incorrect: This option describes Devadana (also called Devadaya).
In this type of grant, land was donated in the name of a deity or temple, and the revenue generated from that land was
used for temple maintenance, performance of daily rituals, festivals, and payment to temple staff such as priests and
attendants.
Option c is incorrect: This refers to Pallichchhanda.
Pallichchhanda denotes land grants made to Jain and Buddhist religious establishments such as monasteries (viharas or
pallis). It represents the non-Brahmanical counterpart of Devadana.
Option d is incorrect: This corresponds to Shalabhoga.
Shalabhoga refers to land grants earmarked for the maintenance of educational institutions, such as schools or centres
of learning. The revenue from such land was used to support teachers, students, and infrastructure.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding the Indian Councils Act, 1861:
I. It empowered the Viceroy to issue ordinances without the concurrence of the Legislative Council during an emergency.
II. It marked the beginning of representative institutions in India by including Indians as nominated members in legislative councils.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Indian Councils Act 1861 was a key constitutional measure enacted by the British Parliament in 1861 during the
tenure of Lord Canning, the first Viceroy of India after the Crown took over administration from the East India Company
under the Government of India Act, 1858.
The Act was a significant constitutional measure enacted by the British Parliament to reorganise governance in India
after the Revolt of 1857. It marked a shift from strict centralisation to a limited form of administrative decentralisation.
Statement I is correct: The Indian Councils Act 1861 empowered the Viceroy (Governor-General) to promulgate
ordinances during emergencies without the concurrence of the Legislative Council. Such ordinances had the force of
law for a limited period (6 months).
This power remains a feature of the Indian Constitution today under Articles 123 (President) and 213 (Governor).
The Act restored the legislative powers of the Presidencies of Bombay and Madras. The Act initiated the process of
decentralization. It reversed the centralizing tendency that had started with the Regulating Act of 1773 and reached its
peak with the Charter Act of 1833 (which had stripped Bombay and Madras of their legislative powers).
Statement II is correct: This Act marked the beginning of representative institutions by associating Indians with the
law-making process. It provided that the Viceroy should nominate some Indians as non-official members of his
expanded council.

[1]

In 1862, Lord Canning nominated three Indians to his legislative council: the Raja of Benares, the Maharaja of Patiala,
and Sir Dinkar Rao.

questions.text: With reference to modern Indian history, which one of the following events took place earliest?
questions.option_a: Formation of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha
questions.option_c: Formation of the Hindustan Republican Association
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Option a is incorrect: Formation of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha (1926)
The Naujawan Bharat Sabha was established in 1926 at Lahore by Bhagat Singh along with other revolutionary
nationalists.
● It functioned as a youth organization aimed at mobilizing young people, peasants and workers against colonial rule,
promoting patriotism, secularism, and revolutionary ideals, and creating a mass base for revolutionary activities,
particularly in Punjab.
● Option b is correct: Formation of the Swaraj Party (1923)
The Swaraj Party was established in January 1923 by Chittaranjan Das and Motilal Nehru following the suspension of
the Non-Cooperation Movement by Mahatma Gandhi in 1922 after the Chauri Chaura incident.
● A section of Congress leaders believed that the nationalist movement should enter the legislative councils created
under the reforms of the Government of India Act 1919 and obstruct colonial governance from within.
● This approach came to be known as the “Council-Entry Programme” and those advocating entry into legislative
councils came to be known as the ‘Swarajists’,
● The Swarajists contested elections to the central and provincial legislatures in 1923 and attempted to expose the
limitations of constitutional reforms by blocking government measures, opposing budgets, and demanding greater
self-government.
Option c is incorrect: Formation of the Hindustan Republican Association (1924)
The Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) was founded in 1924 by revolutionaries such as Ram Prasad Bismil,
Sachindra Nath Sanyal and Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee.
● Its objective was to organize an armed revolutionary struggle against British rule and establish a federal republic in
India.
● The organization later gained prominence through revolutionary actions such as the Kakori Train Robbery (1925).
● In 1928, it was reorganized into the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA).
Option d is incorrect: Establishment of the Simon Commission (1927)
The Simon Commission was appointed in 1927 by the British Government under the chairmanship of John Simon to
review the working of the constitutional reforms introduced by the Government of India Act 1919.
● Since no Indian member was included, it triggered widespread protests across the country with the slogan “Simon
Go Back.”
● During protests against the commission in Lahore in 1928, Lala Lajpat Rai was seriously injured in a police lathi
charge and later died.
Chronologically, the sequence is:
Swaraj Party (1923) → HRA (1924) → Bharat Naujawan Sabha (1926) → Simon Commission (1927)
Therefore, the earliest event is the formation of the Swaraj Party (1923).

questions.text: During Indian freedom struggle, the article "The Cult of the Bomb" was authored by:
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
The famous revolutionary pamphlet “The Philosophy of the Bomb” (1930) was written by Bhagwati Charan Vohra, a
leading member of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA).
● The pamphlet defended the revolutionary use of violence against British colonial rule and articulated the
ideological position of the HSRA.
● It was written as a direct response to the article “The Cult of the Bomb,” authored by Mahatma Gandhi in his
journal Young India in 1930.
● In December 1929, revolutionaries from the HSRA attempted to bomb the train carrying Viceroy Lord Irwin (near
Delhi) as a symbolic protest against British rule, though the Viceroy escaped unharmed.
● In the article “The Cult of the Bomb,” Gandhi strongly criticized revolutionary violence and argued that the use of
bombs and assassinations was morally wrong and politically harmful to the Indian freedom struggle, advocating
instead the path of non-violence (Ahimsa) and Satyagraha.
● In response, Bhagwati Charan Vohra’s pamphlet justified revolutionary action and criticized the exclusive reliance
on non-violence. He defended their methods by saying that these violent methods were as important as Gandhiji’s
non-violent methods.
Thus, “The Philosophy of the Bomb” was written in reply to Gandhi’s article “The Cult of the Bomb.”

questions.text: With reference to medieval India, which one of the following is the correct sequence of administrative units of the Vijayanagara Kingdom in ascending order in terms of size?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Vijayanagara Empire (1336–1646) maintained a highly organized administrative hierarchy to manage its vast
territories in South India. To ensure efficient governance and revenue collection, the empire was divided into several
tiers, ranging from the central capital to the smallest village unit.
In ascending order (from smallest to largest), the hierarchy is as follows:
1. Grama (Village): This was the basic, smallest unit of administration. Each village was managed by a body of local
representatives.
2. Sthala (Sub-district): A group of villages formed a Sthala. It is roughly equivalent to a modern-day sub-district or the
Mughal Pargana.
3. Nadu (District): Multiple Sthalas were grouped together to form a Nadu. This unit is comparable to a modern district
or the Mughal Sarkar.
4. Mandalam (Rajya): This was the largest administrative division, equivalent to a province, modern day state or the
Mughal Suba. The empire was divided into several Mandalams (like Cholamandalam or Jayamkonda Cholamandalam),
each usually governed by a member of the royal family or a high-ranking noble known as a Mandaleshvara.
Therefore, the correct ascending order (smallest → largest) is:

[3]

Sthala → Nadu → Mandalam
Hence, option c) is the correct answer.

questions.text: With reference to modern Indian history, who among the following was the first General Secretary of the All-India Kisan Sabha (AIKS) formed in 1936?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The All-India Kisan Sabha (AIKS) was established in 1936 at the Lucknow Session of the Indian National Congress, 1936
as an organised platform to mobilise peasants and articulate their demands against oppressive agrarian practices such as
high rents, illegal cesses, forced labour, and zamindari exploitation.
I. Swami Sahajanand Saraswati became the first President of the AIKS. He was a prominent peasant leader from Bihar
and had earlier founded the Bihar Provincial Kisan Sabha (1929), which played a major role in mobilising peasants.
II. N. G. Ranga, an important peasant leader from Andhra, was appointed the first General Secretary of the AIKS. He
later emerged as one of the foremost advocates of farmers’ rights in India.
III. Also, a kisan manifesto was issued and a periodical under Indulal Yagnik started.

questions.text: With reference to the Ilbert Bill controversy during British India, which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.option_a: The bill sought to allow Indian judges to try European subjects in criminal cases.
questions.option_b: The Bill was introduced during the tenure of Viceroy Lord Ripon.
questions.option_c: The Bill faced strong opposition from the European community in India.
questions.option_d: The Indian National Congress passed a resolution in favour of the Bill during its annual session.
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Option a is correct: The Ilbert Bill (1833) was introduced to remove racial discrimination in the colonial judicial system.
Under the existing legal arrangement, Indian district magistrates and sessions judges were not permitted to try
European British subjects in criminal cases, even if they were of equivalent rank to European judges.
The bill proposed to empower Indian judges to exercise the same criminal jurisdiction over Europeans.
Option b is correct: The bill was introduced in 1883 during the viceroyalty of Lord Ripon, who is often regarded as one
of the most liberal British administrators in India.
The measure was drafted and placed before the Imperial Legislative Council by Sir Courtenay Ilbert, the Law Member
of the Council.
Lord Ripon supported the proposal as part of his broader policy of administrative reform and greater equality in the
colonial system.
Option c is correct: The proposal provoked intense agitation among Europeans living in India, particularly British
planters, merchants, and officials.
Many Europeans argued that Indian judges were not competent to try European defendants, revealing the deep racial
prejudices prevalent in colonial society.
Public meetings, petitions, and campaigns were organized against the bill, and this agitation came to be described by
some historians as a “White Mutiny.”
Because of this pressure, the government eventually modified the bill before its enactment in 1884.

[4]

Option d is incorrect: The Indian National Congress was established in 1885, whereas the Ilbert Bill controversy took
place in 1883–84.
Therefore, the Congress did not exist at the time of the controversy and could not have passed any resolution
supporting the bill.
Although the episode did help expose racial discrimination in colonial administration and influenced the growth of
political consciousness among educated Indians, it preceded the formation of the Congress by about two years.

questions.text:
With reference to the Communist movement in Modern Indian history, consider the following statements:
I. In October 1920 the Communist Party of India (CPI) was founded at Tashkent.
II. M. N. Roy, S. A. Dange, Muzaffar Ahmad, and P. C. Joshi were CPI’s prominent leaders.
III. The Communist Party of India supported the Quit India Movement of 1942.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement I is correct: The Communist Party of India (CPI) was indeed formed in 1920, in Tashkent (now in Uzbekistan)
by M.N. Roy, Abani Mukherji and others. This was done under the aegis of the Comintern (Communist International) to
organize a revolutionary struggle against British imperialism. In 1925, the Indian Communist Conference at Kanpur
formalised the foundation of the CPI.
Statement II is correct: Leaders such as M. N. Roy, S. A., Muzaffar Ahmad, and P. C. Joshi were central to the growth and
consolidation of the communist movement in India.
Statement III is incorrect: The Communist Party of India did not support the Quit India Movement (1942).
Initially, the CPI had opposed the Second World War, considering it an imperialist conflict. However, after Nazi Germany
invaded the Soviet Union in 1941, the party changed its stance and began supporting the Allied war effort, describing it as
a “People’s War” against fascism. Consequently, the CPI opposed the Quit India Movement and instead urged
cooperation with the British war effort.
Knowledge base:
1. The Meerut Conspiracy Case (1929) was initiated by the British colonial government to curb the spread of communist
ideology and labour activism in India.
A number of trade unionists and communist leaders, such as S. A. Dange, Muzaffar Ahmed, and Shaukat Usmani were
arrested and tried on charges of conspiring to overthrow British rule through revolutionary means. The case drew
international attention and highlighted the growing influence of communist and trade union movements in India.
2. Communists played an important role in mobilizing workers and peasants through mass organizations such as:
● All India Trade Union Congress – founded in 1920, which became a major platform for labour organization and
industrial strikes.
● All India Kisan Sabha – established in 1936 to organize peasants and address agrarian issues such as high rents,
indebtedness, and exploitation by landlords.
Through these organizations, the communist movement significantly influenced labour and peasant politics in the late
colonial period.

questions.text: With reference to Indian architecture, which among the following was the first to be constructed?
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Option d is correct: Karle Caves (Maharashtra)
The Karle (Karla) Caves were constructed around the 2nd century BCE during the Satavahana period.
They represent one of the earliest and largest surviving Buddhist rock-cut caves in India.
Karla cave complex is famous for its “Grand Chaitya”. The Grand Chaitya hall is of apsidal vault roof type with many pillars
and a stupa at the back.
The monument is associated with Hinayana Buddhism, and inscriptions indicate donations from merchants and guilds
connected with the western Deccan trade routes.
Option a is incorrect: Dashavatara Temple, Deogarh (Uttar Pradesh)
The Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh was built during the Gupta period (6th century CE).
It is one of the earliest structural Hindu temples made of stone and is dedicated to Vishnu.
The temple is notable for its Nagara-style architecture, square sanctum, and sculptural panels depicting themes such as
Anantasayana Vishnu.
Option b is incorrect: Chaukhandi Stupa (Uttar Pradesh)
The Chaukhandi Stupa marks the spot where Gautama Buddha is believed to have met his first five disciples before
delivering his first sermon at Sarnath.
The original stupa structure dates to the Gupta period (around the 5th century CE).
The octagonal tower seen today was added in the 16th century during the Mughal period by Govardhan to commemorate
the visit of Humayun.
Option c is incorrect: Udayagiri Caves (Madhya Pradesh)
These are a group of 20 rock-cut caves (primarily Hindu Vaishnava, with one Jain) near Vidisha in Madhya Pradesh.
These caves were excavated in the early 5th century CE during the reign of Chandragupta II.
They represent one of the earliest Hindu rock-cut cave complexes, with notable sculptures like the Varaha (boar)
incarnation of Vishnu relief.
Hence, among the given sites, Karle Caves (Maharashtra), constructed during the 2nd century BCE, is the earliest.

questions.text: Consider the following statements: Statement I: The Constitution of India came into force on January 26, 1950. Statement II: On 26th January 1930, Indians across the country observed ‘Purna Swaraj Day’. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.option_a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
questions.option_b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
questions.option_c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
questions.option_d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement I is correct: The Constitution of India was formally adopted on 26 November 1949 by the Constituent
Assembly of India after nearly three years of deliberations. However, instead of bringing it into force immediately, the
Assembly decided that the Constitution would commence on 26 January 1950.
● During the interim period, some provisions such as those relating to citizenship, elections, and provisional parliament
came into effect on 26 November 1949.
● The entire Constitution became operational on 26 January 1950, marking the transformation of India into a sovereign
democratic republic and the end of the Government of India Act, 1935 as the governing framework.
Statement II is correct: At the Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress in 1929, presided over by Jawaharlal
Nehru, the Indian National Congress adopted the historic Purna Swaraj Declaration, rejecting the earlier demand for
Dominion Status and proclaiming complete independence (Purna Swaraj) as the ultimate goal of the national movement.
● Following this resolution, the Congress called upon Indians across the country to observe 26 January 1930 as
“Independence Day”, with public pledges to work for complete independence from British rule. Hence, on 26th
January 1930, Indians across the country observed Purna Swaraj Day, committing themselves to the goal of
complete self-rule and rejecting dominion status under British governance.
● For several years thereafter, 26 January continued to be commemorated by nationalists as the symbolic day of India’s
freedom struggle.
Why Statement II explains Statement I
When India finally became independent in 1947, the leaders of the Constituent Assembly of India wanted the
commencement of the Constitution of India to retain a historical connection with the freedom movement.
Therefore, even though the Constitution had been adopted on 26 November 1949, they deliberately postponed its
enforcement until 26 January 1950 to commemorate the historic observance of the Purna Swaraj Day on 26th January,
1930.
Thus, Statement II provides the historical reason behind the choice of 26 January for the commencement of the
Constitution.
Therefore, Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.

questions.text:
With reference to ancient Indian philosophies, consider the following information: Philosophy Founder/ Core Idea Proponent
I. Nyaya Jaimini It postulates that the primary means to attain liberation (moksha) is through the valid means of knowledge (pramanas)
II. Vaisheshika Kanada It advocates atomism, holding that all physical objects are reducible to a finite number of atoms
III. Samkhya Kapila It denies the existence of Purusha (consciousne ss) and accepts only material reality (Prakriti)
IV. Yoga Patanjali It emphasizes control of mind and body through the Eightfold Path to attain liberation (moksha). In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Ancient Indian philosophical thought developed through a rich and systematic tradition known as the Darshanas
(schools of philosophy), which sought to understand the nature of reality, knowledge, and liberation (moksha). These
schools broadly emerged during the later Vedic period and were refined over centuries through rigorous debate and
textual traditions.
They are classified into two major categories:
● Orthodox (Astika) schools - These accept the authority of the Vedas. There are six classical systems: Nyaya,
Vaisheshika, Samkhya, Yoga, Mimamsa, and Vedanta.
● Heterodox (Nastika) schools - These reject the authority of the Vedas. Prominent among them are Buddhism,
Jainism, and Charvaka (Lokayata).

[7]

Information in row I is incorrect: Nyaya:
The Nyaya school of philosophy was founded by Gautama, not Jaimini. Jaimini is actually associated with the Mimamsa
school.
● Nyaya primarily deals with logic (Anvikshiki) and epistemology, and it recognizes four valid means of knowledge
(Pramanas): Perception (Pratyaksha), Inference (Anumana), Comparison (Upamana), and Testimony (Shabda).
● Nyaya holds that correct knowledge obtained through valid pramanas leads to liberation (moksha).
Since the founder is incorrectly matched, this pair is incorrect.
Information in row II is correct: Vaisheshika
The Vaisheshika school was founded by Kanada. It is known for its atomistic theory of the universe (Paramanuvada).
It postulated that all objects in the physical universe are reducible to a finite number of atoms. It holds that the smallest,
indivisible, indestructible part of the world is an atom (anu). It argued that all physical things are a combination of the
atoms of earth, water, fire, and air.
Information in row III is incorrect: Samkhya
The Samkhya school was indeed founded by Kapila, but the core idea given is wrong.
It does not deny Purusha; rather, the entire philosophy is built on the interaction between Purusha and Prakriti. It is
dualistic philosophy that views reality to be composed of purusa (consciousness or spirit) and prakriti (nature), both of
which are independent of the other.
Liberation occurs when Purusha realizes its distinction from Prakriti.
Information in row IV is correct: Yoga
The Yoga school was systematized by Patanjali in the Yoga Sutras.
Its central teaching is the control of mind and body to attain liberation through Ashtanga Yoga (Eightfold Path):
1. Yama (ethical restraints)
2. Niyama (observances)
3. Asana (posture)
4. Pranayama (breath control)
5. Pratyahara (withdrawal of senses)
6. Dharana (concentration)
7. Dhyana (meditation)
8. Samadhi (complete absorption)
The aim is chitta-vritti-nirodha (cessation of mental fluctuations), leading to liberation.
Knowledge Base:

questions.text: In which among the following Major Rock Edicts would you find Mauryan Emperor Ashoka’s victory over Kalinga and the subsequent remorse that led him to embrace Dhamma?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Option c is correct: Major Rock Edict XIII of Ashoka provides the most explicit reference to his conquest of Kalinga
during the Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE).
● In this edict, Ashoka describes the devastating consequences of the war, noting that about 100,000 people were
killed, many more died later, and around 150,000 were deported.
● The inscription reveals Ashoka’s profound remorse for the suffering inflicted on the people of Kalinga. He
particularly laments the distress caused to Brahmanas, Shramanas, householders, and other innocent people who
were affected by the war.
● This moral reflection led Ashoka to reconsider the value of military conquest. Consequently, Ashoka declared his
commitment to the policy of Dhamma, emphasizing ethical conduct, compassion, tolerance, and welfare of all beings.
● He renounced the policy of Bherighosha (conquest by war/physical force) and replaced it with Dhammaghosha
(conquest by Dhamma).
● Thus, Major Rock Edict XIII is historically significant because it records both the Kalinga War and Ashoka’s
transformation into a ruler guided by moral and humanitarian principles.
Option a is incorrect: Major Rock Edict I:
This edict primarily deals with the prohibition and regulation of animal slaughter in the Mauryan Empire. Ashoka
emphasizes reducing the killing of animals for royal feasts and encourages greater compassion toward living beings. It
does not mention the Kalinga War.
Option b is incorrect: Major Rock Edict V:
This edict discusses the appointment of Dhamma Mahamatras, special officials responsible for promoting Dhamma,
ensuring social welfare, and looking after various groups such as women, the elderly, and prisoners. It contains no
reference to the Kalinga conquest.
Option d is incorrect: Major Rock Edict VIII:
This edict describes Ashoka’s practice of Dhamma-yatras (moral tours), where the emperor traveled to meet people,
interact with ascetics and Brahmanas, and promote ethical values. It contrasts these tours with earlier royal pleasure
tours but does not refer to the Kalinga War or Ashoka’s remorse.

questions.text:
With reference to the Indian National Movement, consider the following events:
I. Adoption of the Lahore Resolution by the Muslim League
II. Launch of Individual Satyagraha Movement
III. Arrival of the Cabinet Mission to India
IV. Proposal of the C. Rajagopalachari Formula
How many of the above events took place during the tenure of Lord Linlithgow as the Viceroy of India?
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Lord Linlithgow served as the Viceroy of India from 1936 to 1943, holding the longest tenure of any British Viceroy in
India. His viceroyalty coincided with a highly turbulent phase of the Indian national movement and the outbreak of World
War II.

[9]

During his tenure:
● The Government of India Act 1935 was implemented, leading to the establishment of provincial autonomy and the
formation of Congress ministries in several provinces in 1937.
● In 1939, he unilaterally declared India a participant in World War II without consulting Indian leaders, which led to
the resignation of the Indian National Congress ministries.
● His period also witnessed major political developments such as the Lahore Resolution (1940), the Individual
Satyagraha (1940–41), the Cripps Mission (1942), and the launch of the Quit India Movement (1942).
Option I is correct: Adoption of the Lahore Resolution by the Muslim League (1940):
The Lahore Resolution was adopted in March 1940 at the Lahore session of the All-India Muslim League under the
leadership of Muhammad Ali Jinnah. It demanded the creation of independent states for Muslims in the north-western
and eastern regions of India, which later evolved into the demand for Pakistan.
This event took place during Lord Linlithgow’s tenure.
Option II is correct: Launch of Individual Satyagraha (1940):
After the failure of the August Offer (1940), Mahatma Gandhi launched the Individual Satyagraha in October 1940 as a
limited protest against India’s forced participation in World War II without consultation with Indian leaders and also to
assert the right to free speech against British war efforts during World War II. The first satyagrahi was Vinoba Bhave,
followed by Jawaharlal Nehru.
This event also took place during Linlithgow’s tenure.
Option III is incorrect: Arrival of the Cabinet Mission to India (1946):
The Cabinet Mission was sent by the British Government in March 1946, comprising Lord Pethick-Lawrence, Sir
Stafford Cripps, and A.V. Alexander.
Its objective was to negotiate the transfer of power and devise a constitutional framework for India, including the
formation of a Constituent Assembly and a federal structure with groupings of provinces.
However, by this time Lord Linlithgow had already demitted office (1943), and Lord Wavell was the Viceroy. Hence, this
event did not occur during Linlithgow's tenure.
Option IV is incorrect: Proposal of the C. Rajagopalachari Formula (1944)
The C. Rajagopalachari Formula was proposed in 1944 by C. Rajagopalachari.
Purpose of the formula:
● To resolve the political deadlock between the Congress and the Muslim League.
● It suggested that after independence, a plebiscite should be held in Muslim-majority areas to decide whether they
wished to form a separate state.
The proposal formed the basis for the Gandhi–Jinnah Talks between Mahatma Gandhi and Muhammad Ali Jinnah.
However, in 1944 the Viceroy was Lord Wavell, because Lord Linlithgow’s tenure ended in 1943.
Hence, this event falls outside Linlithgow’s period.

questions.text:
Consider the following religious sects of ancient India:
I. Sautrantika
II. Sthanakvasi
III. Vaibhasika
IV. Terapanthi
V. Kapalika VI. Yogacara
How many of the above are sects of Buddhism?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Buddhism, founded by Gautama Buddha around the 6th century BCE, later underwent a major doctrinal division into
Hinayana (Theravada) and Mahayana traditions.

[10]

● Hinayana, which spread mainly in South and Southeast Asia, gave rise to philosophical schools such as Vaibhasika
and Sautrantika.
● In contrast, Mahayana, prevalent in North and Northeast Asia, developed schools like Yogacara (Vijnanavada) and
Madhyamika (Shunyavada).
Option I is correct: Sautrantika
The Sautrantika school is a Buddhist school associated with the Hinayana tradition. It emphasized the authority of the
Sutras (Suttas) rather than the Abhidharma texts, hence the name “Sautrantika” (followers of the sutras). It developed
important philosophical ideas such as momentariness (kshanika-vada) and influenced later Buddhist epistemology.
Option II is incorrect: Sthanakvasi
This is not a Buddhist sect. It is a sub-sect of Svetambara Jainism (a major branch of Jainism). It emerged as a reformist
group that rejected idol worship and temple rituals and focused on internal spiritual practices like meditation, ethical
conduct, and ascetic discipline.
Option III is correct: Vaibhasika
A prominent Buddhist school belonging to the Hinayana tradition. The Vaibhasikas believed in the doctrine that “all
dharmas exist” (sarvam asti) across past, present, and future. They gave great importance to the Abhidharma literature,
especially the Mahavibhasha.
Together, Sautrantika and Vaibhasika represent the major philosophical traditions of Hinayana Buddhism.
Option IV is incorrect: Terapanthi
The Terapanthi sect is another reformist school within Jainism, emerging from the Svetambara tradition in the 18th
century under Acharya Bhikshu. It emphasizes strict discipline, a single religious authority (Acharya), and moral
purification.
Option V is incorrect: Kapalika
The Kapalikas were an extreme Shaiva sect devoted to the worship of Shiva in his fierce forms and associated with
Tantric practices. Kapalikas were known for their extreme asceticism, use of skulls (kapala), cremation-ground rituals,
and transgressive practices aimed at attaining spiritual power.
Option VI is correct: Yogacara
Yogacara, also known as Vijnanavada (the doctrine of consciousness) is an important Mahayana Buddhist philosophical
school. It holds that reality is fundamentally mental and that external objects have no independent existence apart from
consciousness.
Yogacara and Madhyamika are among the major philosophical sects of Mahayana Buddhism.

questions.text:
Consider the following foreign travellers:
I. Niccolo de Conti
II. Francois Bernier
III. Domingo Paes
IV. Fernao Nuniz
V. Abdur Razzak
How many of the above foreign travellers visited the Vijayanagara Empire in medieval India?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Vijayanagara Empire (14th–17th century), founded by Harihara and Bukka in 1336, emerged as a major political and
cultural power in South India.

[11]

● Its capital, Vijayanagara (modern Hampi), became one of the largest and most prosperous cities in the medieval world,
known for its well-planned urban layout, thriving markets, strong military, and temple architecture.
● Much of our knowledge about the empire comes from the accounts of foreign travellers: Niccolo de Conti, Abdur
Razzaq, Domingo Paes, Fernao Nuniz, Duarte Barbosa.
Option I is correct: Niccol de Conti
He was an Italian traveller who visited Vijayanagara in the early 15th century (c. 1420s) during the reign of Devaraya I
(140-1422 CE). His account highlights the immense size of the capital city, dense population, flourishing trade, and strong
military power, indicating the empire’s early consolidation under the Sangama rulers.
Option II is incorrect: Francois Bernier
He was a 17th-century French physician and traveller who stayed in India primarily during the reign of Aurangzeb. His
writings mainly deal with the Mughal Empire; its administration, land revenue system, and social conditions. By this time,
Vijayanagara had already declined after the Battle of Talikota (1565); hence he had no association with it.
Option III is correct: Domingo Paes
A Portuguese traveller who visited the Vijayanagara empire in the early 16th century during the reign of Krishnadeva
Raya (1509-1529).
His account is one of the most detailed and reliable descriptions of the empire. He vividly described the royal court,
military organization, festivals like Mahanavami, bustling markets, and the immense wealth of the kingdom, highlighting
its peak prosperity.
Option IV is correct: Fernao Nuniz
A Portuguese chronicler who visited Vijayanagara in the 16th century. Unlike Paes, he focused more on the historical
narrative of the empire, including:
● Origin and rise of Vijayanagara
● Succession of rulers
● Internal conflicts
His writings complement Paes by providing a historical perspective rather than just a contemporary description.
Option V is correct: Abdur Razzaq
He was a Persian envoy/ambassador and traveller sent by the ruler of Herat (Timurid Empire) to the court of Deva
Raya II of the Vijayanagara Empire in the 15th Century (1440s). His account is one of the earliest detailed foreign
descriptions of Vijayanagara. He famously described Vijayanagara as one of the greatest cities in the world at that time.

questions.text: With reference to Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, which one of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.option_a: He played a leading role in the Nagpur Flag Satyagraha (1923), organizing mass civil resistance in defence of the right to hoist the national flag.
questions.option_b: He earned the title “Sardar” for his leadership in mobilizing peasants during the Kheda Satyagraha (1918).
questions.option_c: He presided over the Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress (1931).
questions.option_d: In the Constituent Assembly, he served as the Chairman of the Advisory Committee on Fundamental Rights, Minorities, and Tribal and Excluded Areas.
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (1875–1950) was a prominent leader of the Indian freedom struggle, a skilled lawyer, and a
statesman known as the “Iron Man of India.”
● He played a key role in organizing peasants during the Kheda (1918) and Bardoli (1928) Satyagrahas, demonstrating
exceptional leadership and organizational skill.
● Patel was instrumental in the integration of over 500 princely states into the Indian Union after independence,
earning him the reputation of a national unifier.

[12]

● He served as India’s first Deputy Prime Minister and first Minister of Home Affairs, and contributed significantly to
the Constituent Assembly.
● He was posthumously awarded the Bharat Ratna (India's highest civilian honor) in 1991.
● His birth anniversary is celebrated as National Unity Day (Rashtriya Ekta Diwas) in India.
Option a is correct: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was put in charge of the Flag Satyagraha (also known as Jhanda
Satyagraha) in Nagpur (1923) by the Congress leadership.
● He led and organized the movement, mobilized volunteers, addressed meetings, and negotiated with authorities.
● The satyagraha involved mass civil disobedience against British restrictions on hoisting the Indian national flag,
with thousands participating, and it eventually led to an agreement allowing peaceful processions.
Option b is incorrect: While Sardar Patel’s political journey began with the Kheda Satyagraha (1918), where he gave up
his thriving legal practice to organize peasants against tax collection during a famine. However, he did not receive the
title "Sardar" then. The title was conferred upon him during the Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928.
● In Bardoli, he led an organized strike against a 22% increase in land revenue.
● His leadership was so impactful that the women of Bardoli began addressing him as "Sardar" (meaning Chief or
Leader), a title Mahatma Gandhi later popularized globally.
Option c is correct: Sardar Patel presided over the Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress (1931). This session
was held after the Gandhi-Irwin Pact.
● It adopted two landmark resolutions:
o Fundamental Rights: Included civil liberties such as freedom of speech, equality before law, and protection of
minorities.
o National Economic Programme: Focused on socio-economic justice, including labour rights, state control of
key industries, and reduction of inequality.
● These resolutions later significantly influenced the Directive Principles and Fundamental Rights in the Constitution.
Option d is correct: In the Constituent Assembly, Sardar Patel served as the Chairman of the Advisory Committee on
Fundamental Rights, Minorities, and Tribal and Excluded Areas. This committee was crucial in designing safeguards for
civil liberties and minority rights.
Additionally, he also served as the chairman of the Provincial Constitution Committee.

questions.text:
With reference to administrative officials of the Gupta period, consider the following pairs: Official Function
I. Dutakas High ranking judicial or military officers
II. Sandhivigrahika Minister in-charge of War and Peace
III. Mahapratihara Commander of infantry
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
The Gupta Empire (c. 4th–6th century CE) is often regarded as the “Golden Age of Ancient India” because of remarkable
achievements in politics, literature, science, art, and economic prosperity. The empire reached its greatest expansion
under Samudragupta and Chandragupta II.

[13]

The administration of the Guptas was monarchical and moderately decentralized. For administrative convenience, the
empire was divided into provinces (Bhuktis) governed by Uparikas, which were further divided into districts (Vishayas)
administered by Vishayapatis. At the local level, village assemblies and headmen played an important role in governance.
Pair I is incorrect: Dutakas
In the administration of the Gupta Empire, the landgrant inscriptions often mention Dutakas who were associated with
the task of implementing gifts when gifts of land were made to brahmanas and others.
In the Gupta administration, High-ranking officials were called Dandanayakas, and Mahadandanayakas were high-
ranking judicial or military officers.
Pair II is correct: Sandhivigrahika
The Sandhivigrahika (or Mahasandhivigrahika) was the Minister for Peace and War. He was a high-ranking official
responsible for sandhi (peace/treaties) and vigraha (war). He managed diplomatic correspondence with other states,
initiated wars, and concluded alliances and treaties.
Pair III is incorrect: Mahapratihara
The Mahapratiara was the chief of palace guards and an important official connected with the royal establishment,
controlling access to the king. While, the commander of infantry and cavalry were designated as Baladhikrita and
Mahabaladhikrita.

questions.text:
With reference to the Delhi Sultanate, consider the following statements:
I. Extensive expeditions were launched towards Khurasan and Qarachil regions during the reign of Iltutmish.
II. The practice of branding horses (dagh) and maintaining descriptive rolls of soldiers (chehra) was introduced during the reign of Firoz Shah Tughluq.
III. The policy of “Blood and Iron” was implemented to strengthen royal authority and suppress rebellions during the reign of Balban.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Delhi Sultanate was a major medieval Indian polity that ruled large parts of the Indian subcontinent from 1206 to
1526 CE with its capital at Delhi.
● It was established after the decline of Muhammad of Ghor’s conquests by his general Qutb-ud-din Aibak, who
founded the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty.
● The Sultanate was ruled by five successive dynasties: the Mamluk (Slave), Khalji, Tughluq, Sayyid, and Lodi
dynasties.
● It played a crucial role in shaping administration, military organization, architecture, and Indo-Islamic culture in
medieval India.
● The Sultanate came to an end in 1526 after the defeat of Ibrahim Lodi by Babur in the First Battle of Panipat,
which marked the beginning of Mughal rule in India.
Statement I Is incorrect: The Khurasan expedition scheme and the Qarachil campaign were actually undertaken during
the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq (1325-1351), not Iltutmish.
● Muhammad bin Tughluq planned an ambitious expedition to conquer Khurasan, for which a large army was
recruited, but the plan was later abandoned.
● He also launched the Qarachil expedition towards the Himalayan region, which ended disastrously due to harsh
terrain and climate.
Iltutmish (1211-1236) did not launch expeditions into Khurasan or Qarachil. In fact, when Chengiz Khan reached the
Indus chasing the Khwarezm prince, Iltutmish wisely avoided confrontation by refusing asylum to the prince.
Statement II is incorrect: The practices of branding horses (dagh) and maintaining descriptive rolls of soldiers (chehra)
were introduced by Alauddin Khalji as part of his military reforms. These measures aimed to prevent corruption, ensure
that soldiers maintained quality horses, and stop false musters. It was not introduced by Firoz Shah Tughluq (1351-1388).
Statement III is correct: Balban (1246-1287) implemented the policy of “Blood and Iron” to restore the declining
authority of the Sultanate. He adopted a highly centralized and authoritarian style of governance, suppressing rebellions

[14]

ruthlessly and curbing the power of the nobility (especially the Chahalgani). He enhanced the prestige of the monarchy
through practices like Sijda (prostration) and Paibos (kissing the feet), thereby reinforcing the absolute authority of the
Sultan.

questions.text:
With reference to the Carnatic Wars during modern Indian history, consider the following statements:
I. The immediate cause of the First Carnatic War was the outbreak of the War of Austrian Succession in Europe.
II. The Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle, which ended the First Carnatic War, resulted in the restoration of Madras to the British by the French.
III. The Third Carnatic War concluded with the Treaty of Seringapatam.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
The Carnatic Wars were a series of three wars fought in the mid-18th century between the British East India Company
and the French East India Company for political and commercial supremacy in South India.
● These wars were mainly fought in the Carnatic region, which was nominally under the Mughal Empire but had
become politically fragmented by that time.
● The conflicts were closely linked to contemporary European wars, such as the War of the Austrian Succession and the
Seven Years’ War.
● Through military alliances with rival Indian claimants and decisive battles such as the Battle of Wandiwash (1760), the
British gradually defeated the French.
● Ultimately, the Carnatic Wars ended with the Treaty of Paris, which effectively ended French political ambitions in
India and marked the beginning of British political dominance in the subcontinent.
Statement I is correct: The First Carnatic War (1740–1748) was essentially an extension of the European conflict
between the British and the French, which was caused by the Austrian War of Succession.
When Britain and France went to war in Europe, their respective trading companies in India (the British East India
Company and the French East India Company) also entered into hostilities in South India.
Statement II is correct: The Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748) marked the end of the First Carnatic War, as well as the
conclusion of the War of Austrian Succession in Europe. Under the terms of the treaty, both Britain and France agreed
to restore their captured possessions. Accordingly, Madras, captured by the French in 1746, was handed back to the
British, while the French received their territories in North America (notably Louisbourg).
Statement III is incorrect: The Third Carnatic War (1758–1763) ended with the Treaty of Paris, not the Treaty of
Seringapatam. The Treaty of Paris ended the global conflict known as the Seven Years’ War. It allowed the French to
retain a few trading settlements like Pondicherry but without fortifications or large armies, effectively ending French
political ambitions in India.
The Treaty of Seringapatam (1792) was signed after the Third Anglo-Mysore War between the British and Tipu Sultan.
and had no connection with the Carnatic Wars.

questions.text:
With reference to the Dharma Shastras, consider the following statements:
I. They were primarily compiled between 6th century CE and 10th century CE.
II. They form a part of ‘Shruti literature’ and are considered to be of divine origin.
III. They deal with duties, rights, laws, social conduct, and ethical guidelines for individuals and society.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.

[15]

Statement I is incorrect: The Dharma Shastras consist of the Dharamsutras and the Smriti Literature mainly. The
Dharmsutras were mainly compiled between 600 BCE and 400 BCE. The Smriti literature was largely compiled during
the post-Mauryan period, roughly between the 2nd century BCE and 3rd century CE. This period witnessed the
consolidation of social and legal norms in response to changing political and social conditions after the decline of the
Mauryan Empire. Important texts such as the Manusmriti, Yajnavalkya Smriti, and Narada Smriti belong broadly to this
phase.
Hence the Dharm Shashtras as a whole were compiled earlier than the 3rd Century CE and not between 6th century CE
and 10th century CE.
Statement II is incorrect: Dharma Shastras are not part of Shruti literature. In the traditional classification of Hindu
sacred texts:
1. Shruti (“that which is heard”) refers to texts believed to be of divine revelation. These are considered apaurusheya
(not of human origin) and eternal. It includes the Vedas and Upanishads.
2. Smriti (“that which is remembered”) refers to texts that are human authored and composed by sages. It includes the
Dharma Shastras, Puranas, and Epics.
Hence, Dharma Shastras belong to the Smriti tradition, not Shruti. They were written by scholars and lawgivers to
interpret Vedic principles and apply them to practical aspects of society such as law, morality, and social duties
The Manusmriti (also known as Manava Dharmashastra) is traditionally attributed to the ancient lawgiver Manu and is
regarded as one of the earliest and most influential Dharma Shastra texts. Composed in metrical verse, it
systematically discusses:
● the duties of the four varnas,
● the stages of life (ashrama system),
● rules of marriage and inheritance,
● duties of kings and administration of justice,
● punishments and judicial procedures.
Because of its comprehensive treatment of social and legal norms, the Manusmriti greatly influenced later legal
traditions and commentaries within the Dharma Shastra tradition. It was the first translated into English by William
Jones in 1794.
Statement III is correct: Dharma Shastras deal with a wide spectrum of social, legal, and ethical aspects, making them
early treatises on law and society. Their subject matter includes:
● Achara (code of conduct): daily duties, rituals, customs
● Vyavahara (legal procedures): courts, evidence, contracts, inheritance, punishment
● Prayaschitta (penance): expiatory rites for sins and violations
● They also elaborate on:
o Duties based on varna (social class) and ashrama (life stages)
o Role and responsibilities of the king (rajadharma)
o Maintenance of social order and moral discipline
Thus, they functioned as normative guides for both individuals and the state, shaping ancient Indian legal and social
frameworks.

questions.text: With reference to government of India’s budget, consider the following data: “The Government’s Revenue Expenditure is ₹70,000 crores and its Capital Expenditure is ₹30,000 crores. The Revenue Receipts amount to ₹50,000 crores, while Non-debt Capital Receipts are ₹10,000 crores. Additionally, the Government reports a Primary Deficit of ₹28,000 crores.” What is the total interest payment made by the Government?
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Budget deficits are central to analysing the fiscal health of the government. Among them, the relationship between Fiscal
Deficit and Primary Deficit is particularly important, as it directly helps us estimate the interest burden of past
borrowings.
First, we will have to calculate Fiscal Deficit. Fiscal Deficit represents the total borrowing requirement of the
government.
Fiscal Deficit = Total Expenditure - (Revenue Receipts + Non-debt Capital Receipts)
Total Expenditure = Revenue Expenditure + Capital Expenditure = 70,000 + 30,000 = ₹ 1,00,000 crores
Total Receipts (excluding borrowings) = Revenue Receipts + Non-debt Capital Receipts= 50,000 + 10,000 = ₹ 60,000
crores
Fiscal Deficit = 1,00,000 – 60,000 = ₹ 40,000 crores
Now, using the relationship between Fiscal Deficit and Primary Deficit we can arrive at the Interest Payment component.
Primary Deficit = Fiscal Deficit – Interest Payments
Rearranging it we get,
Interest Payments = Fiscal Deficit – Primary Deficit
Interest Payments = 40,000 – 28,000 = ₹ 12,000 crores

Deficit Receipts expenditure routine expenses

Receipts + Non-debt Capital requirement health
Receipts)

Deficit interest payments discipline

questions.text:
With reference the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), consider the following actions:
I. Reducing the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR)
II. Purchasing government securities from the open market
III. Reducing the repo rate
IV. Selling Dollars in the open market
How many of the above actions are likely to lead to an increase in the money supply in the Indian economy?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) controls money supply using various quantitative monetary tools. These tools influence
the availability of credit, cost of borrowing, and liquidity in the system. There are 2 major types of tools:
I. Expansionary which increases money supply and
II. Contractionary which reduces money supply
Option I is correct. A reduction in Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) means banks are required to keep less money as reserves
with RBI. This frees up more funds for lending, thereby increasing credit creation and money supply. It is a direct
expansionary measure.

[17]

Option II is correct. When RBI purchases government securities in the open market, it pays money to banks and
financial institutions This increases reserves in the banking system, leading to higher lending capacity and increased
money supply. This is a classic Open Market Operation (OMO) for expansion.
Option III is correct. A reduction in the repo rate lowers the cost at which banks borrow from RBI. This encourages
banks to borrow more and extend more loans, thereby increasing liquidity and money supply in the economy.
Option IV is incorrect. When RBI sells Dollars, it absorbs liquidity from the market.
Banks, institutions or individuals pay money to RBI to buy dollars, reducing available funds and thereby decreasing money
supply

questions.text:
With reference to different models of investment in infrastructure projects in India, consider the following pairs: Model of Description Investment
I. Hybrid 40% of the Project Cost Annuity Model is provided by the (HAM) Government as Construction Support during the construction period and the balance 60% as regular payments over the operations period along with interest thereon to the concessionaire.
II. Engineering, The private contractor Procurement is responsible for and designing, building, and Construction delivering the project, (EPC) with complete funding provided by the government in the construction phase itself
III. Build-Own- Private entity finances Transfer and builds the project (BOT) and then operates the project for a concession period before transferring it to the government
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Infrastructure financing in India increasingly relies on public-private partnership (PPP) models to balance risk,
efficiency, and investment needs. Each model such as EPC, HAM, and BOT among others, differs in terms of who
finances, who builds, who bears risk, and how returns are generated.
Pair I is correct. The Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM) is a mix of EPC and BOT models. Under this, the government typically
funds around 40% of the project cost during the construction period in installments, while the remaining is arranged by
the private player (during the construction period).
Under HAM, the private developer does not depend on toll revenue; instead, it receives fixed annuity payments from the
government for the remaining 60% part of the project cost, linked to performance and maintenance standards. This
reduces traffic risk for the private sector, making it a balanced risk-sharing model.
Pair II is correct. The Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) model is fundamentally a government-funded
model. Here, the private contractor is responsible only for designing, building, and delivering the project, but does not
invest its own money or operate the asset. The government bears the entire financial burden and operational risk.
Pair III is correct. Under the Build-Own-Transfer (BOT) model, a private entity finances, constructs, and operates the
infrastructure project for a specified concession period. During this period, it recovers its investment through user
charges (like tolls) or annuity payments. After the concession period ends, the project is transferred back to the
government. This model places significant risk on the private sector, especially demand or traffic risk.

questions.text:
With reference to the Indian economy, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Outstanding Debt of the Union Government as a percentage of GDP has steadily increased between 2020-21 and 2024-25.
II. Tamil Nadu has the highest Outstanding Debt among State Governments as percentage of State GDP (GSDP).
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement I is incorrect. Outstanding Debt of the Union Government as a percentage of GDP has steadily decreased
between 2020-21 and 2024-25.
Outstanding Debt of Union Government (% of GDP)
2020-21 61.4
2021-22 58.8

[18]

2022-23 58.1
2023-24 57
2024-25 56.2
Statement II is incorrect. Arunachal Pradesh ( around 57 % of GSDP) and not Tamil Nadu ( around 30.3 % of GSDP) has
the highest Outstanding Debt among State Governments as percentage of State GDP (% of GSDP).

questions.text:
With reference to Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) in India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Deposit-taking NBFCs are permitted to issue cheques drawn on themselves.
II. NBFCs are not subject to the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) requirements.
III. A minimum Net Owned Funds of ₹100 crore is a statutory requirement to start business as an NBFC.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) constitute an important segment of India’s financial system, particularly in
expanding credit access beyond the traditional banking network. However, their regulatory design reflects a careful
balance between operational flexibility and systemic stability.
Statement I is incorrect. NBFC in general including deposit-taking NBFCs (NBFC-Ds) are not permitted to issue cheques
drawn on themselves, nor are they integrated into the payment and settlement system. This restriction arises because
payment systems are systemically sensitive, requiring a high degree of regulatory control. Therefore, such functions are
exclusively reserved for banks, ensuring financial stability.
Statement II is correct. NBFCs are not subject to the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) requirement applicable to banks. CRR is a
monetary policy tool used to regulate liquidity created through demand deposits, which NBFCs are not authorised to
accept. As NBFCs do not participate in money creation in the same manner as banks, imposing CRR on them is neither
necessary nor appropriate.
Statement III is incorrect. In terms of Section 45-IA of the RBI Act, 1934, no company can commence or carry on
business of a non-banking financial institution without obtaining a certificate of registration from the Reserve Bank and
without having a Net Owned Funds (NOF) of ₹10 crore with effect from October 01, 2022 (NBFCs seeking registration shall
have NOF of ₹10 crore ab initio, and existing NBFCs have timeline up to March 31, 2027 to attain NOF of ₹10 crore).

questions.text: The Agreement on Climate Change, Trade and Sustainability (ACCTS), recently seen in the news, is an initiative primarily associated with which of the following organizations?
questions.option_a: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC)
questions.option_c: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
The Agreement on Climate Change, Trade and Sustainability (ACCTS) is a first-of-its-kind initiative that aims to use
trade rules to address climate change and environmental challenges. ACCTS tackles fossil fuel subsidies, promotes trade
in environmental goods and services, and establishes innovative eco-labelling standards.
1. Framework and Consistency: While the ACCTS was launched and negotiated by a small group of "like-minded"
countries (New Zealand, Costa Rica, Iceland, and Switzerland), it is explicitly designed to be consistent with the rules

[19]

of the World Trade Organization (WTO). It is considered an "open plurilateral" agreement, meaning it is open for any
WTO member to join if they meet the standards.
2. Key Objectives:
● Eliminating Tariffs: It removes tariffs on hundreds of environmental goods (e.g., solar panels, wind turbines, energy-
efficient machinery).
● Fossil Fuel Subsidies: It is the first legally binding international agreement to contain specific prohibitions on
fossil fuel subsidies, marking a crucial step toward sustainable development.
● Eco-labelling: It seeks to create common guidelines for voluntary eco-labelling to prevent "greenwashing" and
facilitate trade in sustainable products.
The "Pathfinder" Approach: Because reaching a consensus among all WTO members can be slow, these four countries
developed the ACCTS as a "pathfinder" or template. The goal is to demonstrate how trade and environmental policies can
be integrated, eventually encouraging broader adoption within the multilateral WTO system.
Hence, the ACCTS is associated with the WTO.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding the Methane Hydrates:
I. They are ice-like crystalline solids in which methane molecules are chemically bonded with water molecules.
II. They are formed under conditions of high pressure and low temperature.
III. They are considered a potential energy resource and may contain more carbon than all known reserves of coal, oil, and natural gas combined.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Statement I is incorrect: Methane hydrates (also called gas hydrates or clathrates) are ice-like crystalline solids where
methane molecules are physically trapped (encaged) in a lattice of water molecules via van der Waals forces. There is no
chemical bond between the water and the methane; it is a physical entrapment. If the lattice breaks, the gas simply
escapes.
Statement II is correct: They are stable only under specific thermodynamic conditions of high pressure and low
temperature, which are typically found in:
● Deep ocean sediments (continental margins at depths generally >500 m), and
● Permafrost regions (Arctic areas with permanently frozen ground).
These conditions define the Gas Hydrate Stability Zone (GHSZ).
In India, exploration under the National Gas Hydrate Programme (NGHP) has confirmed significant methane hydrate
deposits of biogenic origin, especially in:
● Krishna–Godavari Basin (east coast) – largest and most promising reserves,
● Mahanadi Basin, and
● Andaman offshore region.
These findings indicate substantial potential for future energy extraction.
Statement III is correct: Methane hydrates are considered a major unconventional energy resource because:
● Methane is a cleaner-burning fuel compared to coal (lower CO₂ emissions per unit energy), and
● Global estimates suggest hydrates may contain more carbon than all known reserves of coal, oil, and natural gas
combined.
However, extraction is technologically challenging and poses risks such as methane leakage (a potent greenhouse gas)
and seafloor instability.

questions.text:
Consider the following Biosphere Reserves in India:
I. Cold Desert Biosphere Reserve
II. Panna Biosphere Reserve
III. Manas Biosphere Reserve
IV. Nokrek Biosphere Reserve
V. Agasthyamalai Biosphere Reserve
How many of the above are included in the World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) under UNESCO’s Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme?
questions.explanation:
Option is the correct answer.
The World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) was established in 1971 as part of UNESCO’s Man and the Biosphere
(MAB) Programme. It is an intergovernmental scientific program that aims to establish a scientific basis for enhancing
the relationship between people and their environments.
● The Goal: To balance the conservation of biological and cultural diversity with economic and social development.
● The Concept: Biosphere Reserves are "living laboratories" where scientists and local communities test sustainable
development models.
Biosphere reserves in India:
India has 18 Biosphere Reserves covering 91,425 sq km, with 13 recognized by UNESCO under WNBR.
The first Indian Biosphere Reserve included in WNBR was Nilgiri (2000).
The latest addition is the Cold Desert Biosphere Reserve (2025), which increased India’s total to 13.
Option I is correct: Cold Desert Biosphere Reserve (Himachal Pradesh)
● It represents the Trans-Himalayan cold desert region of Himachal Pradesh, covering Lahaul–Spiti and adjoining
high-altitude regions. It covers the Pin Valley National Park and its surroundings, Chandratal and Sarchu & Kibber
Wildlife Sanctuary.
● It was included in the UNESCO World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) in 2025 (latest Indian addition).
● This designation brings India’s total to 13 UNESCO Biosphere Reserves.
● This is India’s first high-altitude cold desert biosphere reserve and one of the coldest and driest ecosystems in
UNESCO’s WNBR.
Option II is correct: Panna Biosphere Reserve (Madhya Pradesh)
● Located in the Bundelkhand region of Madhya Pradesh, it encompasses Panna National Park and Ken River basin,
which is central to the Ken-Betwa river-linking project.
● The Panna Tiger Reserve is one of the most important sites in the North Central Highlands for protecting the Bengal
tiger.

[21]

● It was included in the UNESCO World Network of Biosphere Reserves in 2020.
Option III is incorrect: Manas Biosphere Reserve (Assam)
● It is located in the state of Assam and includes Manas National Park, which is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1985),
Project Tiger Reserve, and Elephant Reserve.
● It is famous for its role in rhino reintroduction and as a tiger reserve. It is home to the Pygmy Hog (world's smallest
and rarest pig), Golden Langur, and Hispid Hare.
● Despite its high conservation value, the biosphere reserve remains a National Biosphere Reserve and a UNESCO
World Heritage Site, but it has not been inducted into the MAB (WNBR) network.
Option IV is correct: Nokrek Biosphere Reserve (Meghalaya)
● It is located in the state of Meghalaya on the Tura Range, which forms part of the Meghalaya Plateau.
● Nokrek is the highest peak of the Garo Hills at 1,412 metres.
● Highly vulnerable and threatened faunal species in Nokrek include the slow loris, the giant flying squirrel and the
pig-tailed macaque.
● It was included in UNESCO WNBR in 2009, being one of the earlier Indian biosphere reserves to receive this
recognition.
Option V is correct: Agasthyamalai Biosphere Reserve (Kerala–Tamil Nadu)
● Situated in the southern Western Ghats, one of the world’s major biodiversity hotspots.
● Noted for high endemism, especially in medicinal plants and flora, and cultural significance linked to Agastya hills.
● The reserve includes three wildlife sanctuaries, Shendurney, Peppara and Neyyar, as well as the Kalakad
Mundanthurai Tiger Reserve.
● It was included in WNBR in 2016 due to its ecological and conservation importance.

questions.text: The "Great Pacific Garbage Patch", frequently mentioned in the news, is primarily composed of:
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
The Great Pacific Garbage Patch (GPGP) is a vast accumulation zone of marine debris located in the North Pacific
Ocean, formed due to the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, where circulating ocean currents concentrate floating waste.
It is located halfway between Hawaii and California in the Pacific Ocean.
Core Composition:
● It is primarily composed of plastics, especially:
o Microplastics (tiny particles <5 mm formed due to breakdown of larger plastics)
o Fragmented plastic debris (bags, bottles, packaging material, etc.)
● Microplastics dominate in number, making the patch appear like a “soup of plastic particles” rather than a solid mass.
● Larger plastic items (e.g., fishing nets, ropes, crates) contribute significantly to the total mass, even though they are
fewer in number.
Additional Key Facts:
● The patch is not a visible island; it is a diffuse and dispersed zone of suspended debris.
● It contains an estimated ~1.8 trillion pieces of plastic.
● Major materials include polyethylene and polypropylene, which are lightweight and float easily.
● A significant portion of debris comes from abandoned, lost, or discarded fishing gear (ghost nets) along with land-
based plastic waste.

[23]
