Pair II is correct: Mandamus
Literal Meaning: "We command."
● Purpose: It is a command issued by a higher court to a public official, public body, corporation, inferior court, or
government, asking them to perform a public duty that they have failed or refused to perform.
● Mechanism: It is used to ensure that a public duty is carried out by the person or body legally bound to do it.
● Scope: It is only issued for public duties. It cannot be issued against a private individual, the President of India, or
State Governors in their official capacity.
Pair III is incorrect: Prohibition
Literal Meaning: "To forbid."
● Purpose: It is issued by a higher court (Supreme Court or High Court) to a lower court or quasi-judicial body to
prevent it from exceeding its jurisdiction.
● Why the pair was incorrect: In the description, it was stated as "to quash an order already passed." This is factually
incorrect for Prohibition. Prohibition is preventive, it is issued while the case is still pending to stop the lower court
from proceeding. The act of "quashing" an order after it has been finalized is the function of the writ of Certiorari.
● Scope: It is available only against judicial and quasi-judicial authorities. It cannot be issued against administrative
authorities, legislative bodies, or private individuals.
Pair IV is correct: Quo-Warranto
Literal Meaning: "By what authority (or warrant)."
● Purpose: This writ is issued to inquire into the legality of a person's claim to a public office. It is used to prevent the
illegal "usurpation" (wrongful seizure) of a public office by an individual.
● Mechanism: The court asks the person by what authority they are holding the office. If the claim is not well-founded,
the person is ousted from that office.
● Scope: It can be issued only in cases of a substantive public office of a permanent character created by a statute or
by the Constitution. Unlike other writs, it can be sought by any interested person, even if they are not the directly
aggrieved party.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding Personality Rights in India:
I. Personality rights refer to an individual’s right to control the commercial use of attributes such as name, image, voice, signature or likeness.
II. The Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, is the first Indian statute to exclusively define and provide a dedicated legal framework for "Personality Rights."
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement I is correct: Personality rights (also known as "Right of Publicity") refer to the right of an individual to control
and protect the commercial use of their identity or persona.
● These rights typically cover attributes such as name, image, likeness, voice, signature, or other distinctive personal
characteristics.
● The objective is to prevent the unauthorized commercial exploitation of a person’s identity, for example using a
celebrity’s photograph, voice imitation, or likeness in advertisements without consent.
Statement II is incorrect: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 primarily regulates the collection, processing,
and protection of digital personal data and provides rights to individuals (Data Principals) regarding their personal data.
However, the Act does not exclusively define or create a dedicated statutory framework for personality rights.

[51]

● In India, personality rights are not codified in a single statute and have largely evolved through judicial
interpretation, often drawing from principles of privacy, intellectual property, and unfair commercial use.
● They are protected through a combination of Common Law (the tort of 'Passing Off'), the Trade Marks Act, 1999, and
the Copyright Act, 1957.
The legal foundation of personality rights in India is rooted in the Right to Privacy.
● In the landmark Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) case, the Supreme Court held that the Right to
Privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
● Since a person’s identity, image, and personal attributes are closely linked with dignity and privacy, courts have
treated the unauthorized commercial use of such attributes as a violation of an individual’s privacy and personal
autonomy.

questions.text:
With reference to the Fundamental Rights in the Constitution of India, consider the following:
I. Prohibition of 'begar' and other similar forms of forced labor.
II. Prohibition of employment of children below the age of fourteen years to work in any factory
III. Abolition of untouchability and the prohibition of its practice in any form.
IV. Prohibition of traffic in human beings
How many of the above provisions are specifically covered under the 'Right against Exploitation'?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Right against Exploitation, enshrined in Articles 23 and 24 of Part III (Fundamental Rights) of the Constitution, aims
to eliminate all forms of human exploitation and ensure dignity of labour. It prohibits practices such as human
trafficking, forced labour (begar), bonded labour, and child labour in hazardous occupations.
This right is available to both citizens and non-citizens and reflects the constitutional commitment to social justice and
protection of vulnerable sections, particularly women, children, and economically weaker groups.
Option I is correct: Under Article 23, the Constitution explicitly prohibits traffic in human beings, begar, and other
similar forms of forced labour.
● Begar refers to forced labour without payment, historically practiced in many parts of India where landlords or
authorities compelled people to work without wages.
● The expression “other similar forms of forced labour” has been interpreted broadly by the Supreme Court to include
situations where a person is forced to work under coercion, threat, or economic compulsion.
● Even if some remuneration is paid, the labour may still be considered forced if the worker has no real freedom to
refuse the work.
Thus, this protection clearly falls under the Right against Exploitation.
Option II is correct: According to Article 24, the Constitution prohibits the employment of children below the age of
fourteen years in factories, mines, and other hazardous occupations.
Key points:
● The constitutional prohibition does not extend to all forms of employment.
● Children may still legally work in non-hazardous activities, subject to regulatory laws.
● Later legislation such as the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986 (as amended) expanded restrictions,
but the constitutional provision itself is limited to factories, mines, and other hazardous occupations.
Option III is incorrect: The abolition of untouchability is guaranteed under Article 17, which forms part of the Right to
Equality (Articles 14–18).
Key features:
● Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form.

[52]

● Enforcement of any disability arising from untouchability is punishable by law.
● The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 was enacted to implement this provision.
Although this is an important Fundamental Right, it does not fall under the Right against Exploitation (Articles 23–24).
Option IV is correct: Article 23 prohibits “traffic in human beings”, which includes the buying, selling, or movement of
persons for exploitative purposes.
This concept covers practices such as:
● Human trafficking
● Forced prostitution
● Commercial sexual exploitation
● Sale and purchase of persons for labour or other exploitation
In India, laws such as the Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, 1956 were enacted to combat such activities. The trafficking
of women and children for immoral or commercial purposes is therefore a direct violation of Article 23 and falls within
the scope of the Right against Exploitation.
Knowledge Base: Right against Exploitation
Article 23: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
(1) Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and any contravention of this
provision shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
(2) Nothing in this article shall prevent the State from imposing compulsory service for public purposes, and in imposing
such service the State shall not make any discrimination on grounds only of religion, race, caste or class or any of them.
Article 24: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other
hazardous employment.

questions.text:
With reference to the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha, consider the following statements:
I. The date for his/her election is fixed by the President of India.
II. S/he can be removed from office by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Lok Sabha.
III. The 17th Lok Sabha (2019–2024) was the first Lok Sabha since Independence to complete its full five-year tenure without electing a Deputy Speaker.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha is the second-highest presiding officer of the Lower House of Parliament (Lok
Sabha) after the Speaker.
● The office is provided under Article 93 of the Constitution of India, which requires the Lok Sabha to elect both a
Speaker and a Deputy Speaker from among its members.
● The Deputy Speaker is elected by the Lok Sabha from among its members and performs the duties of the Speaker
when the Speaker is absent or when the office of the Speaker is vacant.
● While presiding over the House, the Deputy Speaker exercises the same powers and authority as the Speaker,
including maintaining order and conducting proceedings.
● The Deputy Speaker also continues in office until the dissolution of the Lok Sabha unless he or she resigns or is
removed by a resolution of the House.
Statement I is incorrect: The President of India fixes the date for the election of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha when a
new House is constituted.
However, the date for the election of the Deputy Speaker is fixed by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha under the Rules of
Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha. Therefore, the President has no role in fixing the date for the election
of the Deputy Speaker.
Under Article 93 of the Constitution, the Lok Sabha shall choose two members to be respectively the Speaker and the
Deputy Speaker “as soon as may be.”

[53]

The Constitution deliberately uses this flexible phrase and does not specify any fixed time frame for electing the Deputy
Speaker. This has allowed situations where the election of the Deputy Speaker has been delayed.
Statement II is correct: According to Article 94 of the Constitution, the Deputy Speaker may be removed from office by
a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Lok Sabha, which is known as an effective majority.
Additionally, at least 14 days’ notice must be given before such a resolution can be moved in the House.
Statement III is correct: Traditionally, the Lok Sabha elected a Deputy Speaker soon after the formation of the House.
However, during the 17th Lok Sabha (2019–2024), the post remained vacant throughout the entire term, making it the
first time since Independence that a full Lok Sabha term passed without electing a Deputy Speaker, marking the longest
vacancy of the office. This raised constitutional and procedural discussions regarding the interpretation of the phrase “as
soon as may be” in Article 93.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
I. The Attorney General for India has the right to speak in and take part in the proceedings of both Houses of Parliament.
II. The Attorney General for India forms the part of the Union Executive.
III. The Attorney General for India is generally regarded as the highest law officer of the country.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Statement I is correct: According to Article 88 of the Constitution of India, Every Minister and the Attorney-General of
India shall have the right to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, either House, any joint sitting
of the Houses, and any committee of Parliament of which he may be named a member, but shall not by virtue of this
article be entitled to vote.
However, the Attorney General is not a Member of Parliament; therefore, he does not possess the right to vote in the
House. This provision enables the Attorney General to assist the Parliament with legal clarifications and constitutional
advice whenever necessary.
Statement II is correct: The Union Executive consists of the President, the Vice-President, the Prime Minister, the
Council of Ministers, and the Attorney General for India.
● The office of the Attorney General is provided under Article 76 of the Constitution of India.
● The Attorney General is appointed by the President of India and serves as the chief legal adviser to the Government
of India.
● Since he/she performs functions on behalf of the Government and assists the executive in legal matters, he/she is
considered a part of the Union Executive.
Statement III is correct: The Attorney General is recognized as the highest law officer of the Government of India. His
primary duties include:
● Giving legal advice to the Government of India on matters referred by the President.
● Performing legal duties assigned by the President under Article 76.
● Discharge the functions conferred on him/her by the Constitution or other law.

questions.text:
With reference to the Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India under Article 143 of the Constitution, consider the following statements:
I. The advisory jurisdiction can be invoked in matters involving a dispute arising out of any treaty or agreement executed before the commencement of the Constitution.
II. The advice tendered by the Supreme Court under its advisory jurisdiction is binding on the President of India.
III. The Supreme Court is constitutionally bound to render its opinion on every reference made by the President under Article 143.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
Statement I is correct: The Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India empowers the President of India to
seek the opinion of the Supreme Court of India on specific matters. Article 143 provides two categories of references:
1. Article 143(1): On any question of law or fact of public importance which has arisen or is likely to arise.
2. Article 143(2): On any dispute arising out of any pre-Constitution treaty, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad, or
other similar instrument, which is excluded from the Court’s original jurisdiction. (matters mentioned under the
proviso of Article 131 of the Constitution)
In both situations, the opinion of the Court is advisory in nature and not a judicial pronouncement, and therefore not
binding on the President.
Statement II is incorrect: The opinion given by the Supreme Court under Article 143 is purely advisory and not binding
on the President.
The President may follow or may not follow the Court’s opinion. The purpose of this provision is mainly to provide the
government with an authoritative legal opinion on complex constitutional or legal questions.
Statement III is incorrect: The Supreme Court is not bound to render an opinion in every case. The duty depends on
the category of the reference:
● Category 1 (Public Importance): The Supreme Court "may" tender its opinion. It can refuse to provide an advisory
opinion if it considers the question inappropriate or political in nature (e.g., the Court declined to answer the
reference in the Ram Janmabhoomi case, 1993).
● Category 2 (Pre-Constitution disputes): In this specific case, the Supreme Court "shall" (is bound to) tender its
opinion to the President.

questions.text: With reference to criminal procedure in India, which of the following legal remedies allows a person to apply for bail before being arrested, based on the apprehension of arrest for a non-bailable offense?
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Option b is correct: Anticipatory bail is a pre-arrest legal remedy that enables a person to seek bail in anticipation of
arrest when they have a reasonable apprehension that they may be arrested for a non-bailable offence.
● Under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, this provision is contained in Section 438. The corresponding provision
under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 is Section 482.
● A person who fears arrest may apply to the High Court or the Court of Session seeking a direction that in the event of
such arrest, they shall be released on bail.
● If the court grants anticipatory bail, the person is released on bail immediately upon arrest without being subjected
to prolonged custody.
● While granting anticipatory bail, the court may impose certain conditions, such as requiring the accused to
cooperate with the investigation, not tamper with evidence, or not leave the country without prior permission.

[55]

● Thus, anticipatory bail functions as an important safeguard of personal liberty, protecting individuals from arbitrary
or motivated arrests, while still ensuring that they remain available for investigation and trial.
Option a is incorrect: Regular Bail
Regular bail is sought after a person has already been arrested and is in police or judicial custody. The accused applies
before a competent court seeking release during the pendency of investigation or trial. Since regular bail is available only
after arrest, it does not apply in this case
Option c is incorrect: Interim Bail
Interim bail is a temporary or short-term bail granted for a limited period while the court is considering an application
for regular bail or anticipatory bail. It operates as a temporary protection against arrest or continued custody.
Unlike other forms of bail, interim bail is not expressly defined in statute; rather, it has evolved through judicial practice
and the inherent powers of courts.
Option d is incorrect: Default Bail
Default bail (also known as statutory bail) arises when the investigating agency fails to complete the investigation and
file the charge sheet within the prescribed statutory time limit. In such a situation, the accused becomes entitled to bail
as a matter of right.
● Under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, this right is provided under Section 167(2).
● Under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, the corresponding provision is Section 187(3).
However, default bail is related to delay in investigation after arrest, not to apprehension of arrest.

questions.text: The constitutional provision which states that "The State shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State" is mentioned under which of the following Parts of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Option d is correct: The given provision is enshrined in Article 50 of the Constitution of India, which reads: “The State
shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State.”
Article 50 is placed under Part IV of the Constitution, which deals with the Directive Principles of State Policy (Articles
36–51). These principles are not enforceable by any court (non-justiciable), but they are fundamental in the governance
of the country and it is the duty of the State to apply them while making laws (Article 37).
Substantive significance of Article 50:
● It aims to ensure the independence of the judiciary, which is a basic feature of the Constitution.
● It seeks to remove executive control over judicial functions, particularly at the subordinate judiciary level (district
and lower courts).
● This separation helps ensure impartial administration of justice, free from executive interference.
Option a is incorrect: Part V
Part V of the Constitution of India (Articles 52–151) deals with “The Union” and contains provisions relating to the
structure and functioning of the Union Government.
● It includes provisions relating to the President, Vice-President, Council of Ministers, Parliament, and the Supreme
Court of India.
● While it does contain provisions regarding the higher judiciary (Supreme Court), it does not deal with the principle of
separation of judiciary from the executive in public services.
Option b is incorrect: Part IV-A

[56]

Part IV-A of the Constitution of India contains the Fundamental Duties of citizens, which are listed under Article 51A of
the Constitution of India.
● These duties were added by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 to emphasize the moral obligations of
citizens towards the nation, such as respecting the Constitution, safeguarding public property, and promoting
harmony.
Option c is incorrect: Part III
Part III of the Constitution of India deals with the Fundamental Rights (Articles 12–35).
● These rights guarantee essential civil liberties and protections to individuals, including the Right to Equality, Right to
Freedom, and Right to Constitutional Remedies.
● The provisions in this part are justiciable, meaning they can be enforced by courts.

questions.text: Which one of the following constitutional functionaries holds office during the pleasure of the President of India?
questions.option_b: Members of the Union Public Service Commission
questions.option_d: Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG)
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
The concept of “holding office during the pleasure of the President” in India is derived from the Doctrine of Pleasure,
which originates from British constitutional practice. In the Indian constitutional framework, this doctrine means that
certain constitutional authorities can be removed by the President without a fixed removal procedure such as
impeachment.
● Several constitutional functionaries in India that hold office during the pleasure of the President include the
Governor of a State, the Attorney General for India, and the members of the Council of Ministers (including the
Prime Minister).
Option c is correct: Governor of a State
The Governor of a State holds office during the pleasure of the President of India.
● According to Article 156(1) of the Constitution of India, “The Governor shall hold office during the pleasure of the
President.”
● Although Article 156(3) prescribes a term of five years, this tenure is not absolute. The Governor may be removed
earlier by the President without any formal impeachment procedure.
Thus, the Governor is a constitutional functionary who serves at the pleasure of the President.
Option a is incorrect: Judge of the High Court
A Judge of a High Court does not hold office during the pleasure of the President.
● As per Article 217 “Every Judge of a High Court shall be appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and
seal, and shall hold office, in the case of an additional or acting Judge, as provided in article 224, and in any other case,
until he attains the age of sixty-two years”
● Hence, a High Court judge enjoys security of tenure and cannot be removed at the discretion of the President.
● The removal procedure is similar to that of judges of the Supreme Court, ensuring the independence of the
judiciary from executive interference.
Therefore, High Court judges do not serve at the pleasure of the President.
Option b is incorrect: Members of the Union Public Service Commission
Members of the Union Public Service Commission also do not hold office during the pleasure of the President.
● Under Article 316, members of the UPSC are appointed by the President and hold office for six years or until the
age of 65 years, whichever is earlier.
● Their removal is governed by Article 317, which provides specific safeguards:

[57]

o In cases of misbehaviour, the President must refer the matter to the Supreme Court, which conducts an
inquiry.
o The member can be removed only after the Supreme Court reports that the charges are proved.
● For certain other grounds (such as insolvency, engaging in paid employment outside duties, or infirmity of mind or
body), the President may remove the member.
Therefore, UPSC members do not serve at the pleasure of the President.
Option d is incorrect: Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG)
The Comptroller and Auditor General of India enjoys one of the strongest constitutional safeguards to ensure
independence.
● Under Article 148, the CAG is appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal but cannot be
removed at the President’s pleasure.
● The CAG is provided with the security of tenure and holds office for six years or until age 65 (whichever earlier).
● The Constitution provides that the CAG can be removed only in the same manner and on the same grounds as a judge
of the Supreme Court.

questions.text: With reference to the Indian Parliament, which of the following Bills does not lapse on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha?
questions.option_a: A Bill originating in the Lok Sabha and pending in the Lok Sabha.
questions.option_b: A Bill passed by Rajya Sabha, and pending in the Lok Sabha.
questions.option_c: A Bill passed by the Lok Sabha, and pending in the Rajya Sabha.
questions.option_d: A Bill for which the President has notified his intention to summon a joint sitting of Houses of Parliament.
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
The legislative procedure in the Parliament of India is governed primarily by Article 107 of the Constitution, which deals
with the introduction and passing of Bills and also lays down the general rule regarding the lapse of Bills on the
dissolution of the Lok Sabha.
● According to Article 107, a Bill pending in Parliament lapses on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha, except a Bill which
is pending in the Rajya Sabha but has not been passed by the Lok Sabha.
● However, the Constitution also provides an important exception under Article 108, which deals with joint sittings of
Parliament to resolve a deadlock between the two Houses.
Option d is correct: When a deadlock arises between the two Houses of Parliament, the President may notify a joint
sitting of the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha under Article 108 to resolve the disagreement.
● According to Article 108(5), once the President has notified the intention to summon a joint sitting, the Bill does not
lapse even if the Lok Sabha is dissolved before the joint sitting actually takes place.
● The joint sitting can still be held after the reconstitution of the Lok Sabha.
Thus, a Bill for which the President has already issued a notification for a joint sitting continues to remain alive
despite the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.
Option a is incorrect: A Bill originating in the Lok Sabha and pending in the Lok Sabha:
A Bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses on its dissolution, irrespective of whether it originated in the Lok Sabha or was
transmitted from the Rajya Sabha. This is because the Lok Sabha, being a temporary House, ceases to exist, and all
pending legislative business before it comes to an end.
Option b is incorrect: A Bill transmitted from the Rajya Sabha and pending in the Lok Sabha:
Even if the Bill originated in the Rajya Sabha, once it is pending in the Lok Sabha at the time of dissolution, it lapses.
The stage of consideration in the Lok Sabha is crucial; pending business in this House does not survive its dissolution.
Option c is incorrect: A Bill passed by the Lok Sabha and pending in the Rajya Sabha:

[58]

Such a Bill also lapses on dissolution of the Lok Sabha, as it has not completed the full legislative process. Since the
House in which the bill originated i.e. the Lok Sabha is dissolved, the Bill cannot proceed further and must be
reintroduced.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding the Constitutional provisions related to District Planning Committees in India:
I. There shall be constituted in every State at the district level a District Planning Committee to consolidate the plans prepared by the Panchayats and the Municipalities in the district.
II. All members of the Committee shall be elected by, and from amongst, the elected members of the Panchayats and Municipalities in the district.
III. The Chairperson of the District Planning Committee shall be nominated by the Governor of the State.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
The provisions relating to the District Planning Committee (DPC) are contained in Article 243ZD of the Constitution of
India, which was inserted by the Seventy-Fourth Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992 to strengthen decentralized
planning at the district level.
Statement I is correct: As per Article 243ZD of the Constitution of India, there shall be constituted in every State at the
district level a District Planning Committee to consolidate the plans prepared by the Panchayats and the Municipalities in
the district and to prepare a draft development plan for the district as a whole.
Statement II is incorrect: The Constitution does not provide that all members of the Committee shall be elected by and
from among the elected members of Panchayats and Municipalities.
● According to Article 243ZD of the Constitution of India, not less than four-fifths (4/5th) of the total number of
members of the District Planning Committee shall be elected by and from among the elected members of the
Panchayats and Municipalities in the district, in proportion to the ratio of the rural and urban population.
Thus, the remaining members may be nominated or included in another manner as determined by the State
Legislature.
Statement III is incorrect: The Constitution does not mandate that the Chairperson of the District Planning
Committee shall be nominated by the Governor.
● Under Article 243ZD of the Constitution of India, the State Legislature determines the manner in which the
Chairperson of the District Planning Committee is to be chosen.
Therefore, the mode of selection of the Chairperson varies from state to state, and the Constitution itself does not
prescribe nomination by the Governor.

questions.text:
With reference to Question Hour in the Parliament of India, consider the following statements:
I. The Question Hour is not mentioned explicitly in the Constitution of India.
II. Untarred questions require an oral answer and allow supplementary questions.
III. Questions can be addressed only to Ministers, and not to the private members of Parliament.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option b is the correct answer.
Question Hour is generally the first hour of a sitting of both Houses of Parliament, during which Members of Parliament
(MPs) ask questions to Ministers regarding the administration and functioning of the Government.
● It is a parliamentary convention governed by the Rules of Procedure of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.
● The primary purpose of Question Hour is to hold the executive accountable to the legislature and ensure
transparency in governance.
● Questions can be classified into three types: Starred, Unstarred, and Short Notice Questions (on urgent matters).

[59]

● The Speaker or Chairperson controls the conduct of Question Hour, including the admissibility and order of
questions.
Statement I is correct: Question Hour is not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution of India. It is a parliamentary
device evolved through practice and is governed by the Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business of Lok Sabha and
Rajya Sabha.
Statement II is incorrect: The statement reverses the definitions of Starred and Unstarred questions:
● Starred Questions: These are distinguished by an asterisk. They require an oral answer on the floor of the House,
and because the answer is oral, supplementary questions can be asked.
● Unstarred Questions: These require a written reply, which is laid on the table of the House. Since the answer is
written, no supplementary questions can follow.
Statement III is incorrect: A Question may also be addressed to a Private Member (Under Rule 40 of the Rules of
Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha), provided that the subject matter of the question relates to some Bill,
Resolution or other matter connected with the business of the House for which that Member is responsible. The
procedure in regard to such questions is same as that followed in the case of questions addressed to a Minister with such
variations as the Speaker may consider necessary.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the idea of ‘Constitutional Morality’ in the context of the Indian democratic system?
questions.option_a: Adherence to the literal text and written provisions of the Constitution without recourse to any external judicial interpretations or doctrines.
questions.option_b: Recognition of constitutional supremacy, ensuring that all laws and authorities derive validity from the Constitution.
questions.option_c: Adherence to the core principles of the Constitution such as justice, liberty, equality, fraternity, secularism, and the rule of law etc.
questions.option_d: A doctrine that limits the power of judicial review to only those matters explicitly listed in the written text of the Constitution.
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Option c is correct: Constitutional Morality is a dynamic and transformative judicial doctrine in India that goes beyond
the literal text of the Constitution to embrace its underlying spirit and foundational values.
1. Meaning and Scope
● Spirit over Letter: It refers to adhering to the Constitution's norms and ethical spirit rather than just its literal words.
● Core Values: It is anchored in the core principles of the Constitution: Justice, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity,
Secularism as well as the Rule of Law.
● Counter to Majoritarianism: It acts as a safeguard for individual rights and minority interests against the "fleeting
fancies" or prejudices of the majority (Social Morality).
2. Origin and Evolution
● Dr. B.R. Ambedkar: During the Constituent Assembly debates, Ambedkar emphasized that constitutional morality is
not a "natural sentiment" in India but a value that must be cultivated. He argued that a democratic constitution is
only a "top-dressing" on essentially undemocratic soil, requiring deliberate respect for constitutional forms to
prevent the perversion of administration.
● George Grote: Ambedkar borrowed the term from this British historian, who defined it as a "paramount reverence"
for constitutional forms.
3. Landmark Judicial Applications
The Supreme Court has increasingly used this doctrine to strike down regressive laws or practices:
● Navtej Singh Johar Case (2018): Decriminalized consensual same-sex acts, ruling that Constitutional Morality must
trump Social Morality.

[60]

● Sabarimala Case (2018): Struck down the ban on women entering the temple, holding that gender equality is a core
constitutional value that overrides discriminatory religious customs.
● Joseph Shine Case (2018): Decriminalized adultery, viewing it as a move toward protecting individual autonomy and
gender dignity.
Option a is incorrect: This reflects literalism or textualism, where emphasis is placed only on the written provisions of
the Constitution. Constitutional morality, however, requires going beyond the text to uphold its underlying principles and
transformative vision, especially in evolving social contexts.
Option b is incorrect: This describes constitutional supremacy, meaning that the Constitution is the highest law and all
authorities derive their power from it. While related, it is a structural principle, whereas constitutional morality is a
normative and value-based concept guiding how that power is exercised.
Option d is incorrect: This refers to a narrow or restrictive approach to judicial review, limiting courts to the explicit
text. In contrast, constitutional morality supports a broader interpretative approach, enabling courts to uphold
constitutional values like dignity, equality, and freedom.

questions.text:
Which of the following are the characteristic features of a Tropical Rainforest?
I. Soils are highly acidic and relatively poor in nutrients.
II. Presence of abundant epiphytes and bryophytes on trees.
III. Slow rate of decomposition of organic matter.
IV. Most nutrients are stored in the biomass rather than in the soil.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.
Tropical Rainforests are dense, evergreen forests that occur in regions with high temperatures and heavy rainfall
throughout the year, mainly near the equatorial belt between about 10°–15° latitude north and south of the Equator.
These forests develop in areas where the annual rainfall generally exceeds 2000 mm and the average temperature
remains around 25–28°C with very little seasonal variation.
Due to the consistently warm and humid climate, tropical rainforests support extremely high biological productivity and
biodiversity, making them the most species-rich terrestrial ecosystems on Earth. They are characterized by multi-
layered vegetation (emergent, canopy, understory, and forest floor), dense canopy cover, abundant epiphytes and lianas,
and evergreen broad-leaved trees.
Option I is correct: Tropical rainforests receive very heavy (often >2000 mm annually) and continuous rainfall.
Continuous rainfall causes intense leaching, which washes away soluble minerals such as calcium, potassium, and
magnesium from the soil.
As a result, rainforest soils (commonly lateritic or oxisols) are acidic and nutrient-poor, despite the presence of
extremely dense vegetation. The apparent paradox is explained by rapid nutrient cycling rather than soil fertility.
Option II is correct: The warm, humid, and moist conditions prevailing throughout the year create an ideal environment
for the growth of epiphytes (plants growing on other plants for support, e.g., orchids) and bryophytes (non-vascular
plants like mosses). These plants thrive on tree trunks and branches due to constant moisture availability.
Option III is incorrect: The rate of decomposition in tropical rainforests is very rapid, not slow. High temperature and
moisture promote intense microbial activity, which quickly breaks down organic matter. However, nutrients released are
immediately absorbed by plant roots, preventing their accumulation in the soil.

[61]

Option IV is correct: Due to rapid decomposition and immediate nutrient uptake, most nutrients are stored in the
living biomass (trees, plants, vegetation) rather than in the soil. This makes the ecosystem highly efficient but also
fragile, as removal of vegetation quickly leads to soil degradation.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding the aerosols in the atmosphere:
I. They are completely anthropogenic and are not found naturally.
II. They lead to the destruction of ozone in the stratosphere.
III. They last in the atmosphere for a much shorter time period than carbon dioxide.
IV. They have a net warming effect on earth's atmosphere.
Which of the above given statements are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option d is the correct answer.
Aerosols are small particles suspended in the atmosphere. They are often not or barely visible to the human eye, yet their
impact on climate, weather, health, and ecology are significant.
Statement 1 is incorrect: Aerosols can be released in the atmosphere by both natural as well as the anthropogenic
sources. Atmospheric aerosols consist of solid/aqueous particles suspended in the atmosphere and are typically of sizes
in the range 0.001–10 μm. Aerosols are generated from a wide range of natural and anthropogenic sources Natural
aerosols include sulfate aerosols that are formed from emissions of sulfur dioxide from volcanic eruptions or from
dimethyl sulfide emissions from phytoplankton in the ocean, sea salt particles emitted at the ocean surface, or mineral
dust aerosol that is emitted by the effects of wind erosion on arid land. Anthropogenic aerosols, i.e., those that are
generated from human activities, include sulfate, nitrate, black carbon, and organic carbon aerosols from fossil-fuel
burning, deforestation fires, and burning of agricultural waste.
Statement II is correct: Aerosols also can act as sites for chemical reactions to take place. The most significant of these
reactions are those that lead to the destruction of stratospheric ozone. During winter in the polar regions, aerosols grow
to form polar stratospheric clouds. The large surface areas of these cloud particles provide sites for chemical reactions to
take place. These reactions lead to the formation of large amounts of reactive chlorine and, ultimately, to the destruction
of ozone in the stratosphere.
Statement III is correct: A carbon dioxide molecule has a lifetime of about 100 years in the atmosphere, while an aerosol
particle has an average life expectancy of only about 10 days. Thus, this statement is correct as an aerosol lasts a much
shorter time in the atmosphere than carbon dioxide. They are generally classified as Short-Lived Climate Pollutants
(SLCP).
Statement IV is incorrect: If aerosol particles are not present in the atmosphere of earth, the Earth would be even
warmer than it already is. Aerosol air pollution has made the planet with about 0.7° F (0.4 °C) cooler than it otherwise
would be, according to the 2021 report by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC). Hence aerosol particles
have a net warming effect on earth's atmosphere.
Source: https://www.ncert.nic.in/ncerts/l/kech207.pdf
https://earth.gsfc.nasa.gov/climate/data/deep-blue/aerosols
https://earth.gsfc.nasa.gov/climate/data/deep-
blue/aerosols#:~:text=Sea%20spray%2C%20mineral%20dust%2C%20smoke,from%20volcanoes%20or%20industrial%20
emissions.
https://climate.nasa.gov/news/215/just-5-questions-aerosols/

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding oilseeds production in India:
I. India ranks first in the world in production of oilseeds such as castor, safflower, sesame and niger.
II. Oilseeds occupy the highest share in terms of both acreage and production value among all crops in India.
III. Madhya Pradesh is the largest producer of oilseeds in the country.
IV. In India, nearly three-fourths of the total area under oilseed cultivation is rainfed.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanation:
Option c is the correct answer.

[62]

Statement I is correct: India holds the first position in the world in the production of several minor oilseeds,
particularly castor seed, safflower, sesame and niger seed. These crops are well suited to India’s semi-arid and tropical
climatic conditions. Also, India ranks second in groundnut, third in rapseed-mustard, fourth in linseed, and fifth in
soybean.
Statement II is incorrect: Oilseeds do not occupy the highest share in terms of acreage or production value among
agricultural crops in India. The food grains group (rice, wheat, coarse cereals and pulses) occupies the largest share of
cropped area and production value in Indian agriculture.
● Oilseeds hold the second-highest acreage and production value in Indian agriculture after food grains.
● Nine major oilseeds, groundnut, soybean, rapeseed-mustard, sunflower, sesame, safflower, niger, castor, and linseed,
occupy 14.3% of the gross cropped area.
Statement III is incorrect: Madhya Pradesh is a major oilseed-producing state, mainly due to its large production of
soybean. However, it is not the largest producer of total oilseeds in the country.
● Rajasthan is the largest producer of oilseeds in India, largely because of its dominant production of rapeseed–
mustard, which is one of the most important rabi oilseed crops.
● Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, and Maharashtra together contribute over 77.68% of India’s total oilseed
output.
Among all oilseed crops cultivated in India, soybean contributes the largest share to the total oilseed production.
Soybean accounts for roughly one-third to nearly forty percent of India’s total oilseed output. Major soybean-producing
states include Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra and Rajasthan.
Statement IV is correct: Oilseed cultivation in India is predominantly rainfed. Nearly three-fourths (around 75%) of the
total area under oilseed cultivation depends on rainfall rather than irrigation. This makes oilseed production highly
vulnerable to monsoon variability, which is one of the major reasons behind fluctuations in domestic edible oil
production.

questions.text: With reference to the recently revised coastline data of India, which among the following States has the longest coastline?
questions.explanation:
Option a is the correct answer.
The coastline of India was recently re-measured and revised by the Government of India using modern geospatial
techniques and high-resolution mapping. This exercise was carried out by the National Hydrographic Office in
collaboration with the Survey of India.
● Earlier officially accepted coastline length of India: 7,516.6 km (based largely on older measurements from the
1970s).
● Revised coastline length: 11,098.81 km.
The increase in length occurred because advanced GIS-based mapping captured several small coastal features such as
bays, creeks, inlets, estuaries, and irregular coastal indentations that were not measured earlier.
● While Gujarat remains the state with the longest coastline overall.
● In the revised list, Tamil Nadu has overtaken Andhra Pradesh to become the state with the second-longest coastline
in India.
● Andaman & Nicobar Islands have the longest coastline overall in India
Revised Coastline Length of the Given States
● Tamil Nadu: 1068.69 km
● Andhra Pradesh: 1053.07 km
● Maharashtra: 877.97 km
● West Bengal: 721.02 km
Thus, Tamil Nadu has the longest coastline among the options provided in the question.
